description: The figure presents tensile property curves as a function of carbon content (0–2 wt%) for two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel series: (a) Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC and (b) Fe-20Mn-10Al-xC. Each subplot displays ultimate tensile strength (UTS, magenta squares), yield strength (YS, blue diamonds), total elongation (TE, black triangles), and δ-ferrite fraction (red crosses, labeled δ), with subplot (b) additionally indicating κ-carbide presence. As carbon increases, UTS rises monotonically from approximately 780 MPa to 1100 MPa in (a) and 760 MPa to 1250 MPa in (b), while YS initially decreases slightly in the duplex region then increases sharply upon transitioning to single-phase austenite. TE exhibits a peak (approximately 85% at ~0.8 wt% C for (a); 60% at ~1.0 wt% C for (b)) corresponding to the transition from duplex (δ+γ) to fully austenitic microstructure, subsequently declining with further carbon addition due to increased κ-carbide precipitation. The δ-phase content decreases with increasing carbon, reaching zero at ~0.7 wt% C for Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC and ~1.0 wt% C for Fe-20Mn-10Al-xC, marking the microstructural transition points. These trends demonstrate that carbon content controls phase stability and precipitation hardening, enabling optimization of strength-ductility balance through composition design.
